title: Commercial Roofing in Duncan, SC | Commercial Roofers of Spartanburg
metaDescription: Commercial roofing for Duncan's I-85 distribution centers and industrial parks, with TPO submittals specified to current IECC and manufacturer wind requirements.

html:
Distribution-Center Roof Specifications on Duncan's I-85 Frontage

Duncan's growth has followed the I-85 interchanges directly, with distribution centers and light-industrial parks filling in the frontage on both sides of the highway. Most of what we spec here is large-plate, single-ply roofing on pre-engineered steel, and most of it is procured through a general contractor's bid process rather than a direct owner call.

Bid-Ready Roofing Specifications for Industrial Park Buildings

Owners developing industrial park space along Duncan's I-85 frontage typically issue a formal bid package before selecting a roofing contractor, and that package usually specifies membrane type, thickness, and warranty term rather than leaving those choices open. We respond to those bids with a proposal built directly against the spec: matching mil thickness, insulation R-value to current IECC targets, and a warranty term that meets or exceeds what's called for.

Where a spec is silent on a detail, like edge metal gauge or walkway pad placement around rooftop units, we note our recommended approach in the proposal rather than assuming and pricing it without comment. Bid reviewers in this market generally prefer that a gap be flagged, not filled in silently.

Multi-Building Industrial Park Roof Programs

A number of the industrial parks along the Duncan corridor were built in phases, which means roofs on the same property can be five, ten, or fifteen years apart in age and under different manufacturer warranties. We keep records by building rather than by property when a park has multiple owners contracting separately, since applying one building's maintenance schedule to a mismatched roof next door is a common source of confusion in property management handoffs.

When we're asked to bid reroofing on one building in a multi-building park, we document the adjacent roofs' condition as part of our site visit, since staging, crane access, and material delivery routes usually have to account for buildings that are still occupied and operating next door.

Coordinating Roof Work Around Active Loading Dock Operations

Distribution buildings in Duncan run loading dock schedules around the clock during peak periods, and roof work has to route around that traffic rather than compete with it. We plan crane placement, material staging, and debris removal paths before mobilizing so trailer queuing and dock door access aren't disrupted during a tear-off or recover project.

Noise and vibration from rooftop work can also matter more here than on a typical office building, since some tenants run sensitive conveyor or sortation equipment directly below the roof deck. We ask about that equipment during scoping so the work sequence can be adjusted if needed.

Items We Confirm Before Bidding Duncan Industrial Work

  • Specified membrane type, thickness, and manufacturer on the bid package
  • Insulation R-value against current IECC requirements for the building type
  • Warranty term and whether NDL coverage is required by the owner
  • Existing roof age and condition on adjacent buildings in a multi-building park
  • Loading dock and trailer traffic patterns that affect staging
  • Any sensitive equipment operating directly under the roof deck

Reroofing Occupied Distribution Space

Most of Duncan's distribution buildings can't go dark for a reroof, so the majority of our work here happens in phases sized to keep the building watertight at the end of every shift. We section the roof into daily work areas based on deck size and crew capacity, and we don't leave a section open overnight regardless of forecast, since a distribution tenant's inventory exposure to water damage is usually far higher than the cost of one extra day of staging.

Where the specification calls for a tie-in between old and new membrane on a phased job, we document that tie-in with photos before it's covered, which becomes part of the warranty and closeout package the owner receives at project end.

Insurance carriers covering distribution inventory sometimes ask for a copy of the phasing plan before underwriting a reroof, particularly where rack storage sits close to the roof deck. We keep that plan in a format that can be forwarded directly to a broker or risk manager without additional drafting.

Questions Owners and Facility Managers Ask

Do you bid against a general contractor's formal specification package?

Yes. Most industrial park work in Duncan comes through a bid package that specifies membrane, thickness, insulation, and warranty term. We build our proposal directly against that spec and flag anything the spec leaves open rather than filling in gaps silently.

How do you handle roofing on one building in a multi-building industrial park?

We document that building's roof separately, including its own age, warranty, and condition, and we check adjacent roofs during the site visit since staging and access often depend on buildings that stay occupied next door during the work.

Can reroofing happen without shutting down loading dock operations?

In most cases, yes. We plan crane placement, staging, and debris routes around active dock traffic and phase the work to avoid competing with trailer queuing or dock door use during peak hours.

What happens if a roof section can't be finished by the end of the day?

We size daily work areas to the crew's actual capacity and don't leave a section open overnight. Watertight closure at the end of every shift takes priority over finishing a larger area in one push.

Do you document tie-ins between old and new membrane on phased reroofs?

We photograph every tie-in before it's covered and include that documentation in the closeout package, since it matters for both warranty coverage and any future work on adjacent sections.
